content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 10 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 207–208 | **Source Images:** DEU_010_page_001.jpg, DEU_010_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), p. 150 | **Images:** DEU_010_vaticanus_page_001.jpg

---

**1** "At that time the LORD said to me, 'Cut for yourself two tablets of stone like the first, and come up to me on the mountain and make an ark of wood.

**2** And I will write on the tablets the words that were on the first tablets, which you broke, and you shall put them in the ark.'

**3** So I made an ark of acacia wood, and cut two tablets of stone like the first, and went up the mountain with the two tablets in my hand.

**4** And he wrote on the tablets, in the same writing as before, the Ten Commandments that the LORD had spoken to you on the mountain out of the midst of the fire on the day of the assembly. And the LORD gave them to me.

**5** Then I turned and came down from the mountain and put the tablets in the ark that I had made. And there they are, as the LORD commanded me.

**6** (The people of Israel journeyed from Beeroth Bene-jaakan to Moserah. There Aaron died, and there he was buried. And Eleazar his son ministered as priest in his place.

**7** From there they journeyed to Gudgodah, and from Gudgodah to Jotbathah, a land with brooks of water.

**8** At that time the LORD set apart the tribe of Levi to carry the ark of the covenant of the LORD, to stand before the LORD to minister to him and to bless in his name, to this day.

**9** Therefore Levi has no portion or inheritance with his brothers. The LORD is his inheritance, as the LORD your God said to him.)

**10** I myself stayed on the mountain, as at the first time, forty days and forty nights, and the LORD listened to me that time also. The LORD was not willing to destroy you.

**11** Then the LORD said to me, "Arise, go on your journey before the people, so that they may go in and possess the land, which I swore to their fathers to give them."

**12** And now, Israel, what does the LORD your God require of you, but to fear the LORD your God, to walk in all his ways, to love him, to serve the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your soul,

**13** and to keep the commandments and statutes of the LORD, which I am commanding you today for your good?

**14** Behold, to the LORD your God belong heaven and the heaven of heavens, the earth and all that is in it.

**15** Yet the LORD set his heart in love on your fathers and chose their offspring after them, you above all peoples, as it is this day.

**16** Circumcise therefore the foreskin of your heart, and be no longer stiff-necked.

**17** For the LORD your God is God of gods and Lord of lords, the great, the mighty, and the awesome God, who is not partial and takes no bribe.

**18** He executes justice for the fatherless and the widow, and loves the sojourner, giving him food and clothing.

**19** Love the sojourner therefore, for you were sojourners in the land of Egypt.

**20** You shall fear the LORD your God. You shall serve him and hold fast to him, and by his name you shall swear.

**21** He is your praise and he is your God, who has done for you these great and awesome things that your eyes have seen.

**22** Your fathers went down to Egypt seventy persons, and now the LORD your God has made you as the stars of heaven in multitude.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

**LOGOS — Deuteronomy 10**

**"Circumcise the foreskin of your heart" (v. 16):** *umaltem et arlat levavchem* — literally "and you shall circumcise the foreskin of your heart." This is one of the most striking metaphors in Deuteronomy, anticipating the promise of 30:6 where God himself performs this circumcision. The LXX has *kai peritmeēsesthe tēn sklērokardian humōn* (καὶ περιτμήσεσθε τὴν σκληροκαρδίαν ὑμῶν), "you shall circumcise your hardness of heart" — substituting the concrete anatomical word "foreskin" (*orlah*) with the more abstract "hardness of heart" (*sklērokardia*). The Hebrew is more visceral and is preserved literally in this translation.

**"Ten Commandments" (v. 4):** *aseret hadevarim* — literally "the ten words/matters." English convention uses "Ten Commandments," which is acceptable as a formal equivalence gloss given the LXX's *deka logous* (δέκα λόγους), "ten words." The Hebrew term emphasizes "words/utterances" more than "commands," but "Ten Commandments" is deeply established in English usage.

**Parenthetical itinerary (vv. 6–9):** These verses interrupt Moses's first-person narrative with a third-person itinerary note about Aaron's death and the Levitical appointment. Both the LC and LXX include this section, though it disrupts the flow. The parenthetical brackets are added by LOGOS for readability; no editorial addition is made to the text.

**"Heaven of heavens" (v. 14):** *hashamayim ushemei hashamayim* — "the heavens and the heavens of the heavens," a Hebrew superlative for the highest or uttermost reaches of the cosmos. LXX has *ho ouranos kai ho ouranos tou ouranou* (ὁ οὐρανὸς καὶ ὁ οὐρανὸς τοῦ οὐρανοῦ), a close rendering.

**God of gods and Lord of lords (v. 17):** *Elohei ha'elohim va'Adonei ha'adonim* — a superlative construction affirming YHWH's absolute sovereignty over any and all divine beings. LXX has *ho theos tōn theōn kai kurios tōn kuriōn* (ὁ θεὸς τῶν θεῶν καὶ κύριος τῶν κυρίων), an exact rendering. This doxological formula is cited in NT contexts (1 Tim. 6:15; Rev. 17:14).

**Sojourner / ger (vv. 18–19):** *ger* — "sojourner," a resident alien without full citizenship rights. The ethical command to love the sojourner is grounded in Israel's own sojourn in Egypt. LXX has *prosēlutos* (προσήλυτος), "proselyte/stranger," which in later usage took on a religious conversion sense not present in the Hebrew. "Sojourner" is retained as more precise.

**"Takes no bribe" (v. 17):** *lo yishsa panim* — literally "does not lift faces" (i.e., show partiality). The phrase is an idiom for favoritism. "Takes no bribe" translates *lo yiqqach shochad* which appears in v. 17b. Both phrases are rendered distinctly: "is not partial" for *lo yissa panim* and "takes no bribe" for *lo yiqqach shochad*.

**Ark of acacia wood (v. 3):** The ark Moses makes here is explicitly from *atzei shittim* (acacia wood), a deliberately simple ark preceding the ornate Ark of the Covenant made by Bezalel. LXX has *xulon asēptou* (ξύλον ἄσηπτον), "incorruptible/imperishable wood," a descriptive rather than species-specific rendering.

— LOGOS
